population: Patients with sepsis admitted to the emergency department
notes: Non-survivors showed significantly higher SIC scores on day 2 than survivors (P<0.05). No significant difference between groups on day 1 (P>0.05).

population: Patients with sepsis admitted to the emergency department
notes: Non-survivors showed significantly higher SIC scores on day 4 than survivors (P<0.001). SIC score on day 4 demonstrated more accurate predictive performance than on day 2.

population: Patients with sepsis admitted to the emergency department
notes: SIC score on day 4 was an independent risk factor for 28-day mortality. In non-survivors, SIC scores gradually increased during the first 4 days (P<0.05). In survivors, SIC scores rose on day 2 then fell below day 1 levels by day 4 (P<0.05).

predictor: Sepsis-induced coagulopathy (SIC) score (PT-INR, platelet count, SOFA)
method: Baseline clinical characteristics comparison between survivors and nonsurvivors
notes: SIC scoring system uses PT-INR (0-2 points), platelet count (0-2 points), and total SOFA (0-2 points). Nonsurvivors were older and had more septic shock than survivors. No significant differences in sex, BMI, comorbidities, or infection sites between groups.

predictor: SIC (Sepsis-Induced Coagulopathy) score trajectory over days 1, 2, and 4
method: Generalized estimating equation (GEE) model with simple-effect analysis
effect_size: Non-survivors showed higher SIC scores on day 2 (P<0.05) and day 4 (P<0.001) than survivors; no significant difference on day 1 (P>0.05)
performance: Main effect of group: Wald χ2=23.151, P<0.001; main effect of time: Wald χ2=33.427, P<0.001; interaction effect: Wald χ2=55.008, P<0.001
notes: Non-survivors: SIC scores gradually increased during first 4 days (P<0.001). Survivors: SIC scores rose on day 2 (P<0.05) then fell below day 1 levels by day 4 (P<0.001).

predictor: SOFA score trajectory over days 1, 2, and 4
method: Generalized estimating equation (GEE) model with simple-effect analysis
effect_size: Non-survivors showed higher SOFA scores than survivors at all three time points (P<0.001)
performance: Main effect of group: Wald χ2=33.434, P<0.001; main effect of time: Wald χ2=18.062, P<0.001; interaction effect: Wald χ2=42.390, P<0.001
notes: Non-survivors: SOFA scores continuously increased during first 4 days (P<0.05). Survivors: SOFA scores temporarily increased on day 2 (P<0.05) then decreased below day 1 levels by day 4 (P<0.05).

SIC on day 4 was an independent risk factor for 28-day mortality after adjusting for age, D-dimer, albumin, lactate levels, SOFA score, APACHE II score, and septic shock. SIC scores on days 2 and 4 were significantly higher in non-survivors than in survivors; no significant difference in SIC score on day 1 between survivors and non-survivors

population: Patients with sepsis (excluding those with history of thrombocytopenia and coagulopathy)
performance: Higher specificity than fixed-day SIC scores at all time points; no significant difference from fixed-day SIC scores overall
notes: No significant difference between fixed-day SIC scores and ΔSIC scores in predicting 28-day mortality, but ΔSIC had higher specificity at all time points; combining SIC and ΔSIC scores recommended for better prognostic evaluation

predictor: SIC score trajectory (persistent high vs. decreasing)
method: Time course analysis comparing survivors and non-survivors
notes: SIC scores increased continuously during first 4 days in non-survivors; in survivors, scores began to decrease on day 4 after a temporary increase on day 2. Persistent high SIC scores predict outcomes.

predictor: SOFA score trajectory (persistent high vs. decreasing)
method: Time course analysis comparing survivors and non-survivors
notes: SOFA scores increased continuously during first 4 days in non-survivors; in survivors, scores began to decrease on day 4 after a temporary increase on day 2. Persistent high SOFA scores predict outcomes.
